course_description:
This course traces the political relationship between Indigenous and Non-Indigenous peoples of Australia as an instance of wider global relations among indigenous societies, colonial powers and contemporary national and international regimes and institutions. Students will gain an understanding of government policies and the responses to these practices by Indigenous peoples by critically evaluating the political frameworks and policy responses used to deal with Indigenous-settler relations. The course will consider - among other topics - land, citizenship and identity, self-determination, constitutional recognition, and the governance of Indigenous organisations.

Welcome to POLS2101 - Indigenous Politics and Policy! The rhetoric of crisis tends to accompany how Indigenous affairs is talked about. Politicians and policy makers frequently make bold pronouncements only to have their "new approach"ﾠreworked in subsequent years before the same issues again come to the fore. The course will explore key themes and issues in Australian Indigenous policy and politics including Aboriginal deaths in custody, identity and citizenship, constitutional reform and Indigenous organisations as sites of political and policy engagement. The course will provide you with knowledge and skills to begin to understand the theoretical and cultural perspectives that underpin Indigenous policy, and to undertake critical analysis of past and current policy approaches and prescriptions.

prerequisites: We recommend completing the following courses before enrolling in this one:

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
Additional timetable information
Please Note:
Lectures:
There will be twelve two-hour lectures starting in
WEEK 1.
There are thirteen weeks in the semester but there will be
no lecture in week 10
.
This is an opportunity for you to do independent study. All lectures will be delivered on-campus and audio-recorded by the central UQ systems. Recordings will be available after the lecture via Echo360 and made available on Blackboard Ultra. Lectures contain opportunities for interactive engagement and feedback.
Tutorials:
There will be eight one-hour tutorials starting in
WEEK 3.
There will be
no tutorials in Weeks 1, 2, 8, 10 or 13
. All tutorials will be delivered on campus. They are not recorded. Tutorials are designed to deepen your engagement with the material and support your ability to learn through working with your peers. 10% of your total grade for this course comes from active participation in tutorials, including preparation.
Attendance and participation is central to effective learning in this course as the course explores different ways of thinking and analysing than are usual in political science. Although lecture recordings will be made available, you are
strongly encouraged to attend lectures and tutorials in person,
as they provide the opportunity to ask questions, critically reflect, learn through engagement and receive real-time feedback. This helps you to make connections between ideas, supporting your learning.

learning_outcomes.description: Apply a critical understanding of the history and politics of Indigenous and settler relations up to the present day.
To identify and critically evaluate the assumptions that drive policy frameworks and strategies.
Apply the necessary tools and skills for critical analysis in reading, textual analysis and writing.

Demonstrate that the recognition of and respect for cultural and social diversity is not just a matter of identifying racial categories or cultural practices, but also identifying and reflecting on the values and knowledge systems that inform them.
Grasp and assess the range of arguments and perspectives articulated across the Australian political landscape in relation to issues impacting upon Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples.

assessment_details.title: Video Reflections
assessment_details.task_description: Rationale Our best learning takes place when we reflect on how our existing knowledge is disrupted or extended, affirmed or unsettled and when we are open with ourselves and others about where our knowledge comes from, what it is based on and how we come to know. Becoming aware of ourselves as active agents rather than passive receptors is an important part of critical thinking. This assessment complements the in-class opportunities to reflect on course content and share ideas and insights with one another. Task Video #1 (4 minutes) Video #2 (7 minutes) For each video reflection you will be provided with stimulus questions (see the Video Reflections folder in the Assessments module on Blackboard Ultra). Consider the questions and reflect on yourself in response to them. Write a script to help you keep focussed and on time. Use Echo video on Blackboard Ultra to video yourself. Submit the video by 5pm on the Friday of the relevant week (3 and 13). The video should open with your name and the number of your video (“My name is… this is my first/second.. video reflection”). Record your video in a quiet place. Speak clearly and to camera. Ensure your microphone is not muffled. Stick to the time requirement. More detailed information Refer to the Video Reflections folder in the Assessments module on Blackboard Ultra. Marks available Video #1 - Pass/Fail (5 marks or 0 marks) Video #2 - 20 marks Submission Submit via the appropriate Turnitin link in the Video Reflections folder in the Assessments module on Blackboard Ultra. Use of generative AI and MT in this assessment - NOT PERMITTED The aim of this assessment is to reflect on your own thinking and knowledge and to notice your own responses to the topics discussed in class. The purpose is to deepen your critical engagement with these topics by becoming aware of the things that influence your understanding and approach to them. The marking criteria for this assessment address your ability to reflect rather than the content of your reflection. We want to hear from you . Accordingly, this assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted . Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Assignments for this course will be submitted electronically via Blackboard Ultra and using Turnitin .
In uploading an assignment via Turnitin you are certifying that it is your original work, that it has not been copied in whole or part from another person or source except where this is properly acknowledged, and that it has not in whole or part been previously submitted for assessment in any other course at this or any other university.
When you successfully submit your assessment to Turnitin after previewing the uploaded document (to make sure that you have chosen the correct file), you should see the “Submission Complete!” message. After this, a downloadable Digital Receipt will display on your Assignment Dashboard. It is your responsibility to download the Digital Receipt as proof of submission. Turnitin will not send this receipt to you automatically.
If you don’t see the downloadable receipt on your assignment dashboard, you should regard your submission as unsuccessful.
If the submission was not successful:
Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot). Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again. If you cannot submit again, then email your course coordinator immediately.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Please refer to Applying for an assessment extension - my.UQ - University of Queensland .
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
Video #1 is Pass/Fail. Unless an extension has been granted for this item, failure to submit on time will result in a Fail grade.
Video #2 is marked according to criteria. Unless an extension has been granted for this item, you will lose 10% of the total mark for this item per 24 hour period from 5pm on due date.

assessment_details.title: Short Paper Critiques (250 words each)
assessment_details.task_description: Rationale Indigenous politics and policy do not occur in a vacuum but rather in a dynamic relationship with non-Indigenous ideas and ways of knowing. When enquiring into Indigenous politics and policy it is therefore important to understand how these things are framed by non-Indigenous ideas and knowledges. This assessment task teaches us to see this framing - and how it structures what we know, rather than simply operating within that framing and so replicating its outcomes. This task develops the skills you will need for the Issues Analysis assessment. A short paper critique (SPC) is a highly focussed analysis of a text that addresses its structural framing rather than its content. Unlike other critical analyses short paper critiques do not address the argument made in a text, but identify the framing that allows that argument to be made. Because this task is not easy there will be plenty of opportunity to practice and the feedback you get for each paper will help you improve. Task Complete 5x Short Paper Critiques on the texts listed under Short Paper Critiques in the course Reading List. Submit via Turnitin by the relevant due dates. These papers will be marked and feedback provided to help you improve. Format at the top of the page note your full name and student number and identify the text using correct referencing (see POLSIS Essay Guide) in paragraph 1 use the author's own words to identify the issue/topic and the core argument made about it (125 words). in paragraph 2 use the author's own words to reveal the framing of this issue/topic (125 words). Stick to the format. Write in full sentences (i.e., not in dot point form or abbreviated English). Check your work for spelling and grammatical errors. Cite and reference correctly according to either APA 7th or Chicago footnote ( https://guides/library.uq.edu.au/referencing ). More detailed information Refer to the Short Paper Critiques folder in the Assessments module on Blackboard Ultra. Marks available Each of the 5 Short Paper Critiques is worth 4% for a possible total of 20%. Submission Submit each of the critiques in Word format ( not pdf) via the relevant Turnitin link in the Short Paper Critiques folder in the Assessments module on Blackboard Ultra. Use of generative AI and MT in this assessment - Limited Use Permitted This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies to assist with the 5 formative critiques, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. You may ONLY use AI/MT in this assessment to translate source material into your preferred language (e.g., from English to another language) for the purposes of clarifying your understanding of a text. All other work is to be completed by you, unaided by AI or MT. You must reference any use of AI/MT using the format outlined in the UQ Library Guide for acknowledging and referencing AI/MT use A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot). Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again. If you cannot submit again, then email your course coordinator immediately.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Please refer to Applying for an assessment extension - my.UQ - University of Queensland .
This is a formative assessment that allows students to develop a core skill over 5 instances using feedback from the previous instance. The skill is required for the hurdle assessment and the final assessment item (Issues Analysis). Each of the formative critiques is only 250 words and one is due every two weeks from Week 4 to Week 11. To allow marking and timely feedback extensions are restricted to a maximum of 7 days.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
Unless an extension has been granted, you will lose 10% of the total mark per 24 hour period from 5pm on the due date.

assessment_details.title: Short Paper Critique HURDLE
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Rationale Indigenous politics and policy do not occur in a vacuum but rather in a dynamic relationship with non-Indigenous ideas and ways of knowing. When enquiring into Indigenous politics and policy it is therefore important to understand how these things are framed by non-Indigenous ideas and knowledges. This assessment task teaches us to see this framing - and how it structures what we know, rather than simply operating within that framing and so replicating its outcomes. Task Complete a short paper critique under exam conditions in tutorial 8 (Week 12). A brief but novel text will be provided in the tutorial. This Short Paper Critique will be handwritten and no technology is allowed. Submit your response along with the stimulus text to your tutor at the end of the tutorial. These papers will be marked against the criteria but no feedback will be provided. Format at the top of the page note your full name and student number and identify the text using correct referencing (see POLSIS Essay Guide) in paragraph 1 use the author's own words to identify the issue/topic and the core argument made about it (125 words). in paragraph 2 use the author's own words to reveal the framing of this issue/topic (125 words). Stick to the format. Write in full sentences (i.e., not in dot point form or abbreviated English). Check your work for spelling and grammatical errors. Cite and reference correctly according to either APA 7th or Chicago footnote ( https://guides/library.uq.edu.au/referencing ). More detailed information Refer to the Short Paper Critiques folder in the Assessments module on Blackboard Ultra. Marks available There are 15 marks available. You must pass this hurdle to pass the course, this means achieving a grade of at least 7.5/15 (50%) Submission Submit your hurdle critique to your tutor along with the stimulus text at the end of tutorial #8 (Week 12). Use of generative AI and MT in this assessment - NOT PERMITTED Students will be required to demonstrate competence in this task independent of AI and MT tools. No Al or MT technology will be permitted during the hurdle assessment. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
The Hurdle Short Paper Critique will be completed under exam conditions in tutorial #8 (week 12). You will be presented with a brief but novel text and required to complete a handwritten 250-word SPC on that text during the tutorial (50 minutes). No technology will be allowed. Submit your work with the stimulus text to your tutor at the end of the tutorial.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessment_details.title: Issues Analysis (2500 words)
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Rationale This assessment allows you to explore a particular issue or topic of interest to you and to apply the structural critique we have been learning to a range of different source materials. The format of the assessment is designed to enable you to write a clear and succinct analysis of an issue or topic as it is presented. This skill is useful for both research and practice (e.g., policy, journalism, legal argument...). It will support your critical engagement with Indigenous politics and policy in your chosen professional field. Task Write a 2,500-word analysis of an issue of Indigenous politics and policy. Pick an issue from the list provided (see the Issues Analysis folder in the Assessments module on Blackboard Ultra for details). Research the scholarly literature and/or policy statements and/or the news media on the issue (you should aim to gather at least 8 sources to enable you to make a focus selection). Select 2 of these sources to concentrate on. You can choose sources that appear to present similar or different approaches to the issue. Identify how the issue is presented in these sources and what kinds of actions (if any) are proposed. Look for specific phrases (and accompanying images if relevant) that reveal the framing of the issue in the sources you have selected. Consider how this framing of the issue expresses one or more of the ideas discussed in the course (use the weekly quotes and the self-referencing gaze/displacement diagram as a guide for your thinking). Determine which of these ideas best exemplifies the framing you see in the sources you have selected. Follow the format outlined in the Issues Analysis folder on Blackboard Ultra to complete your analysis. More detailed information Refer to the Issues Analysis folder in the Assessments module on Blackboard Ultra. Marks available This assessment is worth a total of 40 marks. Submission Submit your Issues Analysis in Word format ( not pdf) via the Turnitin link in the Issues Analysis folder on Blackboard Ultra. Use of generative AI and MT in this assessment - Limited Use Permitted Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. You may ONLY use AI/MT in this assessment to translate source material into your preferred language (e.g., from English to another language) for the purposes of clarifying your understanding of a text. All other work is to be completed by you, unaided by AI or MT. You must reference any use of AI/MT using the format outlined in the UQ Library Guide for acknowledging and referencing AI/MT use A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_resources.library_resources: Find the required and recommended resources for this course on the UQ Library website.

learning_activities.topic: Week 1: Introduction and Orientation This week we will be settling into the course and unsettling some of the ideas we might bring with us to the study of Indigenous politics and policy. Please ensure you have viewed the pre-recorded video as this will help by providing some context and direction for this week.

Week 2: Worldviews This week we will discuss worldviews and knowledge as originating in different approaches to being, and what this means when different ways of being, knowing and doing meet. This will help us begin to understand Indigenous politics and policy in the context of an ongoing relationship between different peoples. Please view the short pre-recording to help you engage with the content for this week.
Week 3: Ideas #1: Race, Racism and Racialism This week we will look at the history of our relationship through the story of an Aboriginal family from south east Queensland.
Tutorial 1 Engage in dialogue around the stimulus question from the lecture in Week 2.

Week 4: Ideas #2: Liberalism and Colonialism This week we will look at some of the foundational political ideas Europeans brought to this continent, how they are related and how they continue to shape this place (and us) today.
Tutorial 2 Engage in dialogue around the stimulus question from the lecture in Week 3.

Week 5: Ideas #3: Equality This week we will think about the idea of equality, and what is meant by this. We will look at Aboriginal activism through the documentary Blood Brothers: Freedom Ride (2013).
Tutorial 3 Engage in dialogue around the stimulus question from the lecture in Week 4.

Week 6: Ideas #4: Self-determination This week we will address another idea prevalent in Indigenous politics and policy, that of self-determination, and how this idea is understood and used and to what ends.
Tutorial 4 Engage in dialogue around the stimulus question from the lecture in Week 5.
MID-SEMESTER BREAK Please do schedule a rest this week! Enjoy time with friends or family, and do things that refresh and revive you.

Week 7: Issues & Responses #1: Royal Commission into Aboriginal Deaths in Custody (RCIADIC) 1991 This week we move from looking at ideas to looking at how these ideas are manifest in Indigenous politics and policy in Australia. This week we address the 1991 Royal Commission into Aboriginal Deaths in Custody which was perhaps Australia's most successful royal commission in terms of identifying the causes of high incarceration and deaths in custody among Indigenous Australians.
Tutorial 5 Engage in dialogue around the stimulus question from the lecture in Week 6.

Week 8: Explanations for Policy Failure This week we ask how policy 'fails'. What do we mean by policy failure? How is this determined? We will look at the response when the anticipated outcome of policy is not achieved, and the reasons given for this.

Week 9: Issues & Responses #2: Northern Territory Emergency Responses (NTER) and Closing the Gap (CTG) 2007 In our second week looking at how ideas manifest in Indigenous politics and policy we turn our attention to the 2007 Northern Territory Emergency Response ('The Intervention') and the major strategic policy framework that came out of it, Closing the Gap.
Tutorial 6 Engage in dialogue around the stimulus question from the lecture in Week 8.

Week 10: NO LECTURE or TUTORIAL (Independent Study Week) There is no lecture or tutorial this week. We strongly encourage you to use this time for your own independent study, particularly to begin thinking about your Issues Analysis assessment. Starting early and being clear about the task supports better outcomes. We will be available if you wish to make an appointment to discuss your assignment.

Week 11: Issues & Responses #3: Native Title and The National Apology Returning to how ideas manifest in Indigenous politics and policy, this week we take a look at the Native Title Act , 1993 (Cth), and the National Apology of 2008.
Tutorial 7 Engage in dialogue around the stimulus question from the lecture in Week 9.

Week 12: Issues & Responses #4: Truth, Treaty and Constitutional Recognition One of the ideas and responses considered a 'solution' in Indigenous politics and policy is some form of official recognition of Indigenous peoples. This week we apply our critical thinking to the issues of truth-telling, treaty making and constitutional recognition.

Tutorial 8 - HURDLE ASSESSMENT In this week's tutorial we will complete the hurdle assessment task for this course. Please refer to the Assessment section of this Course Profile and to the Assessment Module on Blackboard for more information.

Week 13: Policy Object(ive)s and Political Relations In our final week together we will have some fun as we reflect on Indigenous politics and policy and our learning in the course. We will watch a short 'mockumentary' and consider the significance of sausages to Australian political relations...
